title: Hymenophyllum stenocladum
intro: Hymenophyllum stenocladum, also known as St. James' Cap, is a small, rare fern species native to New Zealand, characterized by its delicate, lacy fronds. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine, offering potential health benefits such as anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which may aid in wound healing and the treatment of respiratory issues. The bioactive constituents of Hymenophyllum stenocladum include sesquiterpenes, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. Traditionally, the plant has been used to make herbal teas, infusions, and tinctures, although further research is needed to fully understand its therapeutic potential and confirm its safety for human consumption.
taxonomy: Hymenophyllum stenocladum is a species of fern in the family Hymenophyllaceae. It is a type of filmy fern, known for its delicate leaves. The classification of Hymenophyllum stenocladum is as follows: it belongs to the kingdom Plantae, the division Pteridophyta, the class Polypodiopsida, the order Hymenophyllales, and the family Hymenophyllaceae. Within the family Hymenophyllaceae, it is placed in the genus Hymenophyllum. The specific classification of this species is Hymenophyllum stenocladum.

In some cultures, Hymenophyllum stenocladum is used to treat fever, cough, and respiratory issues.
